coredata.dc:title: Excellent luminescence films of excitation-independent carbon quantum dots toward non-rare-earth phosphor-based white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Haobo
Chen, Daqin
Fang, Gaoliang
Yuan, Shuo
Chen, Xiao
Zhong, Jiasong
coredata.dc:description: Currently, fluorescent carbon quantum dots have been extensively studied for their promising application in solid-state lighting. However, most of the reported carbon quantum dots exhibit short-wavelength blue-green emissions and the obtained carbon solid powders usually show weak luminescence due to aggregation-induced emissive quenching. In this work, carbon quantum dots with excitation wavelength independent yellow emission were synthesized by a facile and green hydrothermal route using water as solvent and o-phenylenediamine as carbon source. Structural and spectroscopic characterizations evidenced that N-related defect state was responsible for yellow luminescence. Importantly, the carbon quantum dots embedded solid films were successfully prepared, which exhibited strong blue light absorption and retained intense yellow emission. As a proof-of-concept experiment, carbon polymer monolith was demonstrated to be an alternative to traditional Ce: Y3Al5O12 phosphors as color converter for constructing white light-emitting diode device with improved color rendering index and correlated color temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Light-emitting diode
Luminescence
Optical materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesizing procedure for the Y-CD sample, the proposed structural model and the corresponding TEM, HRTEM images and histogram of size distribution of the as-prepared CDs.

(a) PLE/PL spectra and (b) absorption spectrum of Y-CDs dispersed in aqueous solution. Insets are the corresponding solution and luminescent photographs under UV lamp excitation. (c) Two-dimensional excitation-emission mapping and (d) contour plot of time-resolved data for the corresponding sample.
(a) XPS full-survey and high-resolution XPS (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s spectra of Y-CDs. (e) FTIR spectrum and (f) Raman spectrum of the prepared Y-CD sample.

Photographs of Y-CD solid powder and aqueous solution under irradiation of (a) sunlight and (b) UV (365 nm) lamp. (c) Luminescent photograph of Y-CD polymer monoliths under irradiation of UV lamp. (d) PLE and PL spectra of Ce: YAG phosphor, CD solution and film. (e–g) excitation-emission mappings and (h–j) contour plots of time-resolved data (monitoring emission wavelength: 500–700 nm) for Y-CD films. From top to bottom: the concentration of Y-CDs in PVA film gradually increases from 0.1 to 0.2–0.4 mg/mL.

(a) EL spectra of commercial blue InGaN chip and wLEDs constructed by combining the chip with Y-CD films as color converters (film thickness from top to bottom: 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 mm) and the corresponding emissive photographs driven by 150 mA operating current. Inset (top) schematically illustrates the structure of prototype wLED device. Dependence of (b) CCT, (c) CRI and (d) CIE chromaticity coordinates of wLEDs on CD film thickness. (e) Normalized EL spectra of wLED as a function of operating current (10–150 mA) and the related (f) CCT and (g) CRI versus driving current. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Excellent luminescence films of excitation-independent carbon quantum dots toward non-rare-earth phosphor-based white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials & Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Energy, Fujian Normal University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350117, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials & Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, fluorescent carbon quantum dots have been extensively studied for their promising application in solid-state lighting. However, most of the reported carbon quantum dots exhibit short-wavelength blue-green emissions and the obtained carbon solid powders usually show weak luminescence due to aggregation-induced emissive quenching. In this work, carbon quantum dots with excitation wavelength independent yellow emission were synthesized by a facile and green hydrothermal route using water as solvent and o-phenylenediamine as carbon source. Structural and spectroscopic characterizations evidenced that N-related defect state was responsible for yellow luminescence. Importantly, the carbon quantum dots embedded solid films were successfully prepared, which exhibited strong blue light absorption and retained intense yellow emission. As a proof-of-concept experiment, carbon polymer monolith was demonstrated to be an alternative to traditional Ce: Y3Al5O12 phosphors as color converter for constructing white light-emitting diode device with improved color rendering index and correlated color temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: Benefited from low electric consumption, high brightness and long lifetime, the application of white light-emitting diodes (wLEDs) has led to significant advances in lighting technology [1–4]. Currently, commercial wLEDs are mainly made of blue-emitting InGaN chip with a yellow phosphor Ce3+: Y3Al5O12 (Ce: YAG) [3]. However, this strategy suffers from the shortage of red spectral component of Ce: YAG phosphor and the abundant usage of scarce rare-earth resources [5]. Therefore, developing the alternative phosphor converters that are inexpensive and simple to prepare is highly desirable.

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) represent a new class of nanomaterials showing great merits in terms of facile synthesis, non-toxicity, low-cost and superior optical performance [6–10]. So far, many kinds of CDs have been reported, but most of them exhibit dominant emissions at the blue-green region [11], which limits their practical applications. Therefore, it is a huge challenge to fabricate CDs with intense long-wavelength emission [12–16]. Additionally, compared to the CDs dispersed in solution, carbon solid-state powders exhibit low photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) owing to the effect of aggregation-induced emission quenching [17–21]. As a consequence, there are few reports on CDs-based wLEDs so far, where only pure CD powders or films are used as color converters [22,23]. In the present work, we have successfully prepared long-wavelength emitting yellow CDs (Y-CDs) through a facile hydrothermal route using water as solvent and o-phenylenediamine (oPD) as carbon source. The obtained CDs show an excitation-independent luminescence and can be easily converted into CD films as color converters to apply in solid-state lighting.

Synthesis of Y-CDs. 250 mg oPD was first dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water and stirred to form a transparent solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After heating at 180 °C in an oven for 2 h and cooling to room temperate naturally, yellow suspensions were achieved. Finally, the solution was purified by the 0.45 μm dialysis membrane and transferred into a vacuum freeze drier for drying 2 days to obtain carbon solid powders.

Preparation of CD Films. A slightly modified procedure reported by Yu et al. was adopted to prepare the Y-CD film [24]. Firstly, a selected Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) of 2.0 g was mixed with 10 mL of water solution (Y-CD contents: 0.1–0.4 mg/mL) and stirred for 30 min. Then the resulting mixture was transferred into a clean glass substrate and naturally dried for 3 days under room temperature to get a solid composite film monolith.

Construction of Y-CD-Based wLEDs. wLED devices were constructed by directly coupling the as-fabricated Y-CD films with different thicknesses on the InGaN blue chip. Opaque silica gels were filled around the edges of the devices in order to avoid the leakage of blue light.

Characterization. Microstructure observation was performed on a JEOL JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 200 kV accelerating voltage. Topographic image of the as-prepared CDs was obtained using an atomic force microscope (AFM, Agilent 5500). The actual compositions of CD samples were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a VG Scientific ESCA Lab Mark II spectrometer equipped with two ultra-high-vacuum 6 chambers. All the binding energies were referenced to the C1s peak of the surface adventitious carbon at 284.6 eV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer in the range 400–4000 cm−1 using the KBr pellet technique. Raman spectra were recorded on a microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer (Renishaw Trade Co., Ltd., England) under the excitation of a 633 nm laser. Absorption and emission spectra were recorded on an Edinburgh Instruments (EI) FS5 spectrofluorometer equipped with continuous (150 W) and pulsed xenon lamps. Excitation-emission mappings of Y-CDs were recorded by continuously changing the excitation wavelength with a fixed step of 1 nm, and the offset between excitation wavelength and emission was set at 30 nm to reduce scattering light. Absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), defined as the ratio of emitted photons to absorbed ones, was determined by a spectrofluorometer (FS5) equipped with an integrating sphere. Time-resolved spectra of Y-CDs were performed on a fluorescent lifetime spectrometer (LifeSpec-II, EI) based on a time-correlated single-photon-counting technique under the excitation of 375 nm picoseconds laser. Electroluminescence spectra, Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates, color rendering index (CRI), correlated color temperature (CCT), and luminous efficiency (LE) of the constructed wLEDs were recorded in a HAAS-2000 spectroradiometer (Everfine) under an operating current of 10–200 mA. All the experiments were performed at room temperature.

As schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 , a facile hydrothermal reaction is adopted to prepare Y-CDs. In this process, oPD acting as carbon source could assemble into fluorescence carbon dots in aqueous solution via dehydrolysis and carbonization. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1a) demonstrate that Y-CDs are monodispersed with homogeneous size distribution of 2–3 nm and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) micrograph confirms its crystalline feature with well-resolved lattice fringes. Evidently, the (100) crystalline plane of CD with the corresponding interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm is clearly marked (Fig. 1). AFM topographic image of CDs coated glass coverslip is recorded and a height profile across an individual CD gives a height of ∼1.5 nm (Fig. S1b). Considering ∼1 nm roughness of the glass coverslip, the height of CDs can be estimated to be about 2.5 nm, being close to the diameter measured by TEM. Combining TEM and AFM measurement, it is proposed that the as-prepared products are carbon quantum dots.

The impact of solvents (DMF, ethanol, formamide, methanol, water) on emission wavelength of the synthesized CDs was further investigated. When water is replaced by other solvents, the emissions of CDs shift toward short-wavelength (Fig. S2), indicating that water in the hydrothermal reaction is a good solvent for the formation of CDs with long-wavelength emission. Photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of Y-CDs are provided in Fig. 2 a. A broad yellow-orange emission band centered at 570 nm is detected in the PL spectrum and multiple excitation bands in the ultraviolet–visible region are observed from PLE spectrum. Notably, similar multi-state absorption is found in the corresponding absorption spectrum (Fig. 2b). As a consequence, the CD aqueous solution shows yellow color and emits bright yellow light upon the irradiation of UV lamp. Furthermore, the hydrothermal reaction conditions, including temperature, time and oPD content, were examined. The optimal reaction temperature/time and oPD content are determined to be 180 °C/2 h and 250 mg (Fig. S3). The absolution PLQY for the present Y-CDs reaches as high as 25–35%.

Impressively, the present Y-CDs exhibit excitation wavelength insensitive luminescence, as evidenced in Fig. 2c and Fig. S4a. Although the emitting intensity is changed, the peak position does not significantly alter upon the variation of excitation light wavelength, indicating that the luminescence of Y-CDs is probably originated from an individual emitting state. As demonstrated in the PL decay curves (Fig. S4b) and the related contour plot (Fig. 2d), no obvious change of decay contour was found with increase of detecting emitting wavelength from 500 nm to 700 nm for Y-CDs. The decay lifetime is determined to be ∼2.5 ns Additionally, similar results can be found for the Y-CD aqueous solutions with different CD contents (Fig. S5). The gradual red-shift of emission wavelength with increase of CD content in solution is due to reabsorption effect. All these results further verify the singe-state emissive feature for the prepared Y-CDs.

To further investigate the surface states and chemical nature of the as-prepared Y-CD sample, FTIR, Raman and XPS characterizations are carried out. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 a) indicates the existence of C, N, O for Y-CD with atomic percentages of 53.0%, 29.6% and 17.4%, respectively. The high resolution C1s XPS spectrum can be fitted into two peaks at 284.6 eV and 285.7 eV (Fig. 3b), which correspond to CC/CC and CO/CN, respectively [25]. The N1s XPS spectrum around 398.4 eV and 399.9 eV are assigned to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively [26] (Fig. 3c). The O1s XPS spectrum could be decomposed into two peaks at 531.1 eV and 532.3 eV, indicating the presence of CO and CO groups, respectively [27] (Fig. 3d). In the FTIR spectrum, peaks at 1645, 1596, and 1350 cm−1 are attributed to CN/CO, CC, and CN stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 3e) [28–31]. The peaks between 2850 and 2960 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching vibrations of methyl/methylene [32]. The broad absorption bands in the range of 3150–3400 cm−1 indicate the presence of amino (NH2) and hydroxyl (OH) functional groups on the surface of the Y-CDs [15]. Moreover, Raman spectrum in the 1000–2000 cm−1 region (Fig. 3f), recorded under 633 nm laser excitation, shows two vibration bands, where the G band (∼1550 cm−1) is attributed to the graphitic sp2 carbon and the D band (∼1350 cm−1) is assigned to the surface state of carbon [33], confirming the existence of the disordered structures or defects on the surface of the present CDs. These results indicate that Y-CDs consist of graphite structures in the core and amorphous carbon on the surface, which are covered with N and O contained heterocycle chemical structures and abundant amino and hydroxyl functional groups (inset of Fig. 1). Consequently, the absorption/excitation bands in the UV region are proposed to be due to the HOMO→LUMO (π→π∗) transition in sp2 C domain, while the bands in the visible region are ascribed to the transitions from π ground states to O and N-related defect states on the surfaces of CDs (Fig. S6). Generally, the reported carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials show two different kinds of emission features. One is excitation-dependent emission, i.e., the emission wavelength gradually changes with variation of excitation wavelength [34]. This is usually attributed to the coexistence of several emitting states, such as sp2 carbon core, O-related defect states and N-related defect states. As a comparison, the other is excitation-independent emission, i.e., the emission wavelength remains unchanged with increase of excitation wavelength, where only one kind of emitting state contributes to emission of CDs. For the present sample, the N-related defect state is proposed to be responsible for its excitation-independent luminescence. As schematically illustrated in Fig. S6, the electrons in the π∗ and O defect states can non-radiatively relax to the N-related defect state to produce long-wavelength yellow-orange luminescence. To gain more insight into the emitting feature, pH-dependent luminescence of Y-CD sample was studied, as exhibited in Fig. S7. Apparently, the visible absorption band gradually shifts toward short-wavelength with increase of pH from 1 to 13, while the emission intensity enhances with increase of pH from 1 to 10 and then remarkably decreases with further increase of pH to 13. These results evidence that the yellow emission of CDs is indeed produced from the surface defect-states and the introduce of H+ or OH− imposes great effect on these surface emitting states.

As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a and b, different to the case of CD solution, the CD powders do not show any visible luminescence upon the irradiation of UV lamp due to the aggregation-induced quenching, certainly limiting their application in wLEDs. To solve this problem, we creatively fabricate freestanding films of Y-CDs using PVA as supporting matrix. Y-CD/PVA films with controllable thickness, shape and size can be easily obtained on a large-scale and maintain intense yellow luminescence, as demonstrated in Fig. S8 and Fig. 4c. PL/PLE spectra of Y-CD solution and film are provided in Fig. 4d. Compared to CD solution, the luminescence of CD film exhibits slight blue-shift, probably owing to the modification of defect-related energy states via interaction between PVA and CDs (Fig. S6). Importantly, the excitation band in blue light region for CD film significantly enhances, being beneficial to apply in InGaN blue chip excitable wLEDs. As a comparison, PLE/PL spectra of traditional Ce: YAG phosphor are also presented. Evidently, both excitation and emission of the prepared CD film resemble those of the commercial Ce: YAG phosphor, confirming that the Y-CD film is a potential alternative to Ce: YAG as non-rare-earth color converter. Furthermore, the impact of CD content in PVA film on optical performance was investigated, as shown in Fig. 4e and j. Apparently, the excitation wavelength independent yellow luminescence is remained for the Y-CD films; however, it is found that the emission gradually shifts toward long-wavelength with increase of CD content in PVA (Fig. 4e and g) owing to the re-absorption effect of CDs. Correspondingly, the decay lifetime is monotonously prolonged with increase of emitting wavelength and increasing CD content in PVA indeed reduces the decay rate of electrons from excited state to ground one, as verified in the contour plots of time-resolved data (Fig. 4h and j).

Finally, as a proof-of-concept experiment, light-emitting devices were designed by coupling Y-CD films with an InGaN chip to demonstrate their potential application in solid-state-lighting. Electroluminescence (EL) spectra show a blue emission band of InGaN chip and a broad yellow emission band of Y-CD film (Fig. 5 a), certainly verifying that the as-synthesized Y-CD polymer monoliths are blue-light-excitable and suitable as yellow color converter for non-rare-earth phosphor-based wLED. As evidenced in Fig. 5b and d, the modification of CD film thickness can finely tune correlated color temperature (CCT, 4000 K-8000 K), color rendering index (CRI, 70-80) and color coordinates for the constructed Y-CD film-based wLEDs. Additionally, the influence of driving current on optical performance of a typical wLED with CD film thickness of 1.5 mm was studied (Fig. 5e and g). As revealed in EL spectra, CD emission intensity relative to blue one of InGaN chip gradually decreases as the forward current increases from 10 to 100 mA (Fig. 5e), indicating that the present CD film exhibits slight saturation absorption effect toward the incident blue light. Fortunately, the EL spectra tend to be stable when the operating current is within 100–150 mA (Fig. 5e) and the related CCT and CRI (Fig. 5f and g) show no remarkable variation with increase of driving current. Specifically, the photoelectric performance of the designed Y-CD film-based wLED can be optimized, exhibiting a bright white light with luminous efficiency of ∼45 lm/W, CRI of ∼78, CCT of ∼5400 K and color coordinates of (0.353,0.371).

In summary, N-doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal reaction using oPD as C/N source and water as solvent. These carbon quantum dots exhibited intense excitation-independent yellow emission with absolute PLQY of 25–35%. TEM observation, PL/PLE/XPS/FTIR/Raman/time-resolved spectra and pH-sensitive emissions evidenced that the observed yellow emission was originated from N-related defect-state on the surfaces of carbon quantum dots. Furthermore, yellow emissive films were fabricated by incorporating carbon quantum dots into PVA matrix. Benefited from intense blue light absorption and bright yellow luminescence, the as-prepared carbon solid films were demonstrated to have promising applications in non-rare-earth phosphor-based wLEDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LR15E020001), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51572065), and 151 Talent's Projects in the Second Level of Zhejiang Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

Figs. S1–S8. Extra TEM/AFM images, PL/PLE spectra, absorption spectra, decay curves, excitation-emission mappings, contour plots of time-resolved data, emissive mechanism and luminescence photographs of carbon quantum dots and polymer monolith.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.06.059.
